concepts.title: Explaination of inverse of a function
concepts.conceptBody:
Inverse of a function

Let $\text{f}:\text{A}\to \text{B}$ be a one-one and onto function then there exists a unique function. 
 

$\text{g}:\text{B}\to \text{A}$ such that $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{y}\Leftrightarrow \text{g}\left( \text{y} \right)=\text{x},\forall \text{x}\in \text{A}$ and $\text{y}\in \text{B}$.

Then $g$ is said to be inverse of $f$.

Thus, $\left. \text{g}={{\text{f}}^{-1}}:\text{B}\to \text{A}=\{\left( \text{f}\left( \text{x} \right),\text{x} \right)]\left( \text{x},\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right) \right)\in \text{f} \right\}$

Let us consider one-one function with domain $\text{A}$ and range $\text{B}$.

Where $\text{A}=\left\{ 1,2,3,4 \right\}$ and $\text{B}=\left\{ 2,4,6,8 \right\}$ and $\text{f}:\text{A}\to \text{B}$ is given by $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=2\text{x}$, then

write $\text{f}$ and ${{\text{f}}^{-1}}$ as a set of ordered pairs.

Here, member $\text{y}\in \text{B}$ arises from one and only one member $\text{x}\in \text{A}$.

So, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }f=\left\{ \left( 1,2 \right),\left( 2,4 \right)\left( 3,6 \right)\left( 4,8 \right) \right\}$

and $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{f}}^{-1}}=\left\{ \left( 2,1 \right)\left( 4,2 \right)\left( 6,3 \right)\left( 8,4 \right) \right\}$

Note in above function

Domain of $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}=\left\{ 1,2,3,4 \right\}=$ range of ${{\text{f}}^{-1}}$

Range of $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}=(2,4,6,8\}=$ domain of ${{\text{f}}^{-1}}$.

Which represents for a function to have its inverse it must be one-one onto or (bijective).

concepts.title: Methods of finding the inverse of a bijective function
concepts.conceptBody:
Method to find Inverse 

  • First of all we have to check whether the function is bijective, i.e. one-one & onto both, or not.
  • If the function is bijective, then for $y=f\left( x \right)$ get
  1. $x=f\left( y \right)$
  2. put $\text{x}$ as ${{\text{f}}^{-1}}\left( \text{y} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left\{ {} \right.$ as $\left. \text{y}=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\Rightarrow {{\text{f}}^{-1}}\left( \text{y} \right)=\text{x} \right\}$
  3. replace $\text{y}$ by $\text{x}$ on right hand side

This is your inverse function

We can also use the following formula for finding the inverse.

$\text{f}\left[ {{\text{f}}^{-1}}\left( \text{x} \right) \right]=\text{x}$

concepts.title: Finding on Inverse of function
concepts.conceptBody:
Finding an Inverse of function

Q) If $f:\left[ 1,\infty \right)\to \left[ 2,\infty \right)$ is given by $f\left( x \right)=x+\frac{1}{x}$ then find ${{f}^{-1}}\left( x \right)$. (assume bijective). 

Solution : 

Let $y=f\left( x \right)$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & ~\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y=\frac{{{x}^{2}}+1}{x}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{x}^{2}}-xy+1=0 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=\frac{\text{y}\pm \sqrt{{{\text{y}}^{2}}-4}}{2} \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{f}}^{-1}}\left( \text{y} \right)=\frac{\text{y}\pm \sqrt{{{\text{y}}^{2}}-4}}{2}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left\{ \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ as }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{y}\Rightarrow \text{x}={{\text{f}}^{-1}}\left( \text{y} \right) \right\} \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{f}}^{-1}}\left( \text{x} \right)=\frac{\text{x}\pm \sqrt{{{\text{y}}^{2}}-4}}{2} \\ \end{array}$

Since, range of inverse function is $\left[ 1,\infty \right)$, therefore, neglecting negative sign, we have,

${{f}^{-1}}\left( x \right)=\frac{x+\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}-4}}{2}$

Let $\text{f}:\mathbf{R}\to \mathbf{R}$ be defined by $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\frac{{{\text{e}}^{\text{x}}}-{{\text{e}}^{-\text{x}}}}{2}$. Is $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ invertible? If so, find its inverse. 

Solution : Let us check for invertibility of $f\left( x \right)$ :

(a) One-one : Here, $\text{{f}'}\left( \text{x} \right)=\frac{{{\text{e}}^{\text{x}}}+{{\text{e}}^{-\text{x}}}}{2}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{f}'\left( x \right)=\frac{{{e}^{2x}}+1}{2{{e}^{x}}}$ which is strictly increasing as ${{e}^{2x}}>0$ for all $x$

Thus, one-one.

(b) Onto : Let $\text{y}=\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}=\frac{{{\text{e}}^{\text{x}}}-{{\text{e}}^{-\text{x}}}}{2}$ where $\text{y}$ is strictly monotonic.

Hence, range of $f\left( x \right)=\left( f\left( -\infty \right),f\left( \infty \right) \right)$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ range of $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\left( -\infty ,\infty \right)$

So, range of $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=$ co-domain.

Hence, $f\left( x \right)$ is one-one and onto.

(c) To find ${{\text{f}}^{-1}}:\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}=\frac{{{\text{e}}^{2\text{x}}}-1}{2{{\text{e}}^{\text{x}}}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{e}}^{2\text{x}}}-2{{\text{e}}^{\text{x}}}\text{y}-1=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{e}}^{\text{x}}}=\frac{2\text{y}\pm \sqrt{4{{\text{y}}^{2}}+4}}{2}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}=\text{log}\left( \text{y}\pm \sqrt{{{\text{y}}^{2}}+1} \right)$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{f}}^{-1}}\left( \text{y} \right)=\text{log}\left( \text{y}\pm \sqrt{{{\text{y}}^{2}}+1} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ {} \right.$ as $\left. \text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{y}\Rightarrow \text{x}={{\text{f}}^{-1}}\left( \text{y} \right) \right]$

Since, ef ${{~}^{-1\left( x \right)}}$ is always positive.

So, neglecting negative sign.

Hence, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{f}^{-1}}\left( x \right)=\text{log}\left( x+\sqrt{{{x}^{2}}+1} \right)$

Q) Let $f:\left[ 1/2,\infty \right)\to \left[ 3/4,\infty \right)$, where $f\left( x \right)={{x}^{2}}-x+1$. Find the inverse of $f\left( x \right)$. 

Hence, solve the equation ${{x}^{2}}-x+1=\frac{1}{2}+\sqrt{x-\frac{3}{4}}$ 

Solution : 

(a) $f\left( x \right)={{x}^{2}}-x+1$

$\Rightarrow \text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)={{\left( \text{x}-\frac{1}{2} \right)}^{2}}+\frac{3}{4}$ which is clearly one-one and onto in given domain and co-domain.

(b) Thus, its inverse can be obtained.

Let $f\left( x \right)=y$

$\Rightarrow y={{\left( x-\frac{1}{2} \right)}^{2}}+\frac{3}{4}$

$\Rightarrow \text{x}-\frac{1}{2}=\pm \sqrt{\text{y}-\frac{3}{4}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{x}=\frac{1}{2}\pm \sqrt{\text{y}-\frac{3}{4}}\left[ \text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{y}\Rightarrow \text{x}={{\text{f}}^{-1}}\left( \text{y} \right) \right]$

$\Rightarrow {{\text{f}}^{-1}}\left( \text{y} \right)=\frac{1}{2}+\sqrt{\text{y}-\frac{3}{4}}$ [neglecting - ve sign as always + ve.]

$\Rightarrow {{\text{f}}^{-1}}\left( \text{x} \right)=\frac{1}{2}+\sqrt{\text{x}-\frac{3}{4}}$

(c) To solve : ${{\text{x}}^{2}}-\text{x}+1=\frac{1}{2}+\sqrt{\text{x}-\frac{3}{4}}$, as $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)={{\text{f}}^{-1}}\left( \text{x} \right)$ has only one solution.

$\begin{align} & i.e.,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,f(x)=x \\ & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{{x}^{2}}-x+1=x \\ & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,{{x}^{2}}-2x+1=0\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,{{(x-1)}^{2}}=0 \\ \end{align}$

$\text{x}=1$ is the required solution.

concepts.title: Properties of Inverse of a function
concepts.conceptBody:
Properties of inverse of a function 

  1. The inverse of bijection is unique.
  2. The inverse of bijection is also bijection.
  3. If $\text{f}:\text{A}\to \text{B}$ is bijection and $\text{g}:\text{B}\to \text{A}$ is inverse of $\text{f}$.

Then fog $={{\text{I}}_{\text{B}}}$ and gof $={{\text{I}}_{\text{A}}}$.

where, ${{\text{I}}_{\text{A}}}$ and ${{\text{I}}_{\text{B}}}$ are identity functions on the sets $\text{A}$ and $\text{B}$ respectively.

  1. If $\text{f}:\text{A}\to \text{B}$ and $\text{g}:\text{B}\to \text{C}$ are two bijections, then gof $:\text{A}\to \text{C}$ is bijection and (gof) ${{)}^{-1}}=$ $\left( {{f}^{-1}}\text{o}{{\text{g}}^{-1}} \right)$.
  2. fog $\ne $ gof but if, fog $=$ gof then either ${{f}^{-1}}=g$ or ${{g}^{-1}}=f$ also.

$\left( \text{fog} \right)\left( \text{x} \right)=\left( \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ gof }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \right)\left( \text{x} \right)=\left( \text{x} \right)\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

concepts.title: Differentiation of inverse of a function(Considering the differentiation of the original function is known)
concepts.conceptBody:
Differentiation of inverse of a function(Considering the differentiation of the original function is known)

Q) Let $g\left( x \right)$ be the inverse of $f\left( x \right)$ and ${f}'\left( x \right)=\frac{1}{1+{{x}^{3}}}$. Then find ${g}'\left( x \right)$ in terms of $g\left( x \right)$. 

Solution : We know, if $g\left( x \right)$ is inverse of $f\left( x \right)$

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ g}\{\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\left( \text{x} \right) \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ {g}'}\left( \text{f}\left( \text{x} \right) \right),\text{{f}'}\left( \text{x} \right)=1 \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{g}'\left\{ f\left( x \right) \right\}=\frac{1}{{f}'\left( x \right)}=1+{{x}^{3}} \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ {g}'}\left\{ \text{f}\left( \text{g}\left( \text{x} \right) \right) \right\}=1+{{(\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right))}^{3}} \\ {} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ {g}'}\left( \text{x} \right)=1+{{(\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right))}^{3}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left[ \therefore \text{f}\left( \text{g}\left( \text{x} \right) \right)=\text{x} \right] \\ \end{array}$
